1: Chapter 19 presents the second potter-and-clay sign-act — but with a crucial difference from chapter 18. Thus says YHWH, Go, buy a potter's earthenware flask, and take some of the elders of the people and some of the elders of the priests. The key distinction: chapter 18 used unfired clay, which can be reshaped. Chapter 19 uses a baqbuq — a fired, glazed ceramic flask. Once pottery has been fired in the kiln, it cannot be reshaped; it can only be broken. The two sign-acts together present two theological moments: the still-reformable and the now-irreversible.
2: And go out to the Valley of the Son of Hinnom at the entry of the Potsherd Gate, and proclaim there the words that I tell you. The location is theologically loaded: the Valley of Hinnom (Gei Ben-Hinnom) was the site of the worst apostasy in Judah's history — child sacrifice to Baal and Molech at the Topheth installation (2 Kings 23:10; Jeremiah 7:31; Jeremiah 32:35). It was also the city's rubbish dump, where refuse and broken pottery (potsherd gate) were discarded. This valley became the NT Gehenna — the garbage-and-fire valley that Jesus uses as the image of final judgment (Matthew 5:22, 29–30; Jeremiah 18:9). The sign-act is performed at the place most associated with the worst of Judah's sin.
3: And say, Hear the word of YHWH, O kings of Judah and inhabitants of Jerusalem. Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Behold, I am bringing such disaster upon this place that the ears of everyone who hears of it will tingle. The audience includes the royal house and the general population — both are to witness the sign-act and receive the oracle. The ears of everyone who hears will tingle (tetzalnah oznav) — the same phrase used in 1 Samuel 3:11 for the announcement of Eli's house's judgment and in 2 Kings 21:12 for Manasseh's judgment. The phrasing links this announcement to the full tradition of radical divine judgment in Israel's history.
4: Because they have forsaken me and have profaned this place by making offerings in it to other gods whom neither they nor their fathers nor the kings of Judah have known; and because they have filled this place with the blood of innocents. The dual indictment: offerings to unknown gods (foreign cults whose identities YHWH disclaims — neither he nor the patriarchs authorized them) and blood of innocents (child sacrifice). The Hinnom Valley is the physical location of both crimes — the place where foreign altars were erected and where children were burned. The crimes are spatially concentrated, making the valley itself the primary locus of covenant violation.
5: And have built the high places of Baal to burn their sons in the fire as burnt offerings to Baal, which I did not command or decree, nor did it come into my mind. The triple denial — did not command, did not decree, did not enter my mind — is one of the strongest possible disavowals. YHWH is emphasizing that child sacrifice was never a possibility within the Yahwistic covenant framework; it is not an extreme form of legitimate worship but a total departure from it. This directly contradicts any theology that portrayed child sacrifice as the ultimate gift to the deity.
6: Therefore, behold, days are coming, declares YHWH, when this place shall no more be called Topheth, or the Valley of the Son of Hinnom, but the Valley of Slaughter. The renaming of the Hinnom Valley as the Valley of Slaughter (gei hahareguah) is a prophetic name-change that anticipates its transformation from a place where children were killed as sacrifice to a place where the population of Jerusalem will be killed as judgment. The location of the worst covenant violation becomes the location of the worst covenant punishment — a spatial covenantal irony.
7: And in this place I will make void the plans of Judah and Jerusalem, and will cause their people to fall by the sword before their enemies, and by the hand of those who seek their life. I will give their dead bodies for food to the birds of the air and to the beasts of the earth. The making void (vevaqqoti) plays on baqbuq (the flask) — a wordplay the Hebrew audience would catch: I will empty out the plans of Judah like one pours out a flask. The unburied bodies for birds and beasts is the covenant-curse standard (Deuteronomy 28:26; Jeremiah 7:33) — this time enacted at the very place where bodies were previously burned as offerings.
8: And I will make this city a horror and a thing to be hissed at. Everyone who passes by it will be horrified and will hiss because of all its wounds. The Jerusalem-as-spectacle theme (cf. Jeremiah 18:16) is intensified: the city that was YHWH's chosen dwelling place, the pride of all the earth (Lamentations 2:15), will become a spectacle of horror. The hissing of passersby is the ancient equivalent of public condemnation — a sound and gesture associated with responses to moral atrocity and divine judgment in the ancient world.
9: And I will make them eat the flesh of their sons and their daughters, and everyone shall eat the flesh of his neighbor in the siege and in the distress, with which their enemies and those who seek their life afflict them. Siege cannibalism — the most extreme form of human suffering under prolonged blockade — is listed in Deuteronomy 28:53–57 as a specific covenant curse for covenant violation. It was historically fulfilled during the Babylonian siege of Jerusalem (Lamentations 4:10: the hands of compassionate women have boiled their own children). The horror of the sign-act location (Hinnom Valley) is compounded by this prediction: the parents who burned their children in the valley will be reduced to eating their children during the siege.
10: Then you shall break the flask in the sight of the men who go with you. The moment of the sign-act itself. The breaking of the fired clay flask is irreversible — unlike the unfired clay of chapter 18, which could be remolded, a broken ceramic vessel cannot be repaired. The witnesses (elders of the people and priests, v1) see it happen. The physical event embodies the theological claim: the moment for reshaping has passed. Jerusalem is now the fired flask, not the unfired clay.
11: And shall say to them, Thus says YHWH of hosts: So will I break this people and this city, as one breaks a potter's vessel, so that it can never be mended. Men shall be buried in Topheth because there will be no place else to bury. The explicit interpretation: as one breaks a potter's vessel, so that it can never be mended. The irreversibility is stressed. The burial reference is grim: so many will die that the Hinnom Valley itself — previously unusable as a burial site because of its cultic impurity — will have to be pressed into service as a cemetery. The place of child sacrifice becomes a mass grave for adults.
12: Thus will I do to this place and its inhabitants, declares YHWH, making this city like Topheth. The houses of Jerusalem and the houses of the kings of Judah shall be defiled like the place of Topheth, with all the houses on whose rooftops offerings have been made to all the host of heaven, and drink offerings have been poured out to other gods. The defilement-logic: the rooftop altars throughout Jerusalem (cf. 2 Kings 23:12; Jeremiah 32:29) have made every house ritually contaminated, extending the Topheth-defilement across the entire city. The worship of the host of heaven (stars, planets) was a Mesopotamian import that competed with and supplemented YHWH worship throughout the monarchy period.
13: The extension of the defilement judgment to every house in Jerusalem (v12) means no quarter of the city is exempt. Even the royal palace — whose rooftop had hosted astral altars installed by Manasseh (2 Kings 21:5) and temporarily dismantled by Josiah (2 Kings 23:12) — is included in the indictment. The thoroughness of the judgment reflects the thoroughness of the apostasy: it extended from the Hinnom Valley to the city center to the royal palace to every neighborhood rooftop.
14: Then Jeremiah came from Topheth, where YHWH had sent him to prophesy, and he stood in the court of the YHWH's house and said to all the people. Jeremiah brings the Hinnom Valley message into the Temple precincts — the most sacred space in Jerusalem. The movement from Topheth to the Temple court is itself symbolic: the place of worst apostasy and the place of YHWH's dwelling are brought into confrontation. The prophet's body carries the message from one sacred site to the other.
15: Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Behold, I am bringing upon this city and upon all its towns all the disaster that I have pronounced against it, because they have stiffened their neck, refusing to hear my words. The summary verdict returns to the stiff-neck diagnosis (cf. Jeremiah 17:23) — the physical metaphor for obstinate refusal to accept the covenant yoke. The disaster pronounced against Jerusalem is comprehensive: this city and all its towns — not just the capital but the entire urban network of Judah. The chapter ends with the covenant sentence pronounced and the sign-act completed: the flask is broken; the judgment is sealed.
